author: @samuelfellows6923
text: 1:24 - 😄 “washing machines”? Er…. 100mm extractor fans ~ toilet, bathroom, utility room, cooker hoods and vented tumble dryer. If I had designed it - it would have the “5 flap” 100mm gravity vents [they do exist] and the turbine would be at the bottom as the air will blow down - how it comes out of these flap vents

author: @JLieppinen
text: I wonder how much the wind that comes from the lower flaps is going to be directed out from the upper flaps due to the direction of the pressure going upwards and the flaps are opening in a way that it would direct the wind from below to hit the upper flap and be directed outside. I don't know if what I wrote is comprehensible, but my suggestion is try this same but block the top and add the fan generator to the bottom with some legs for the tower and then compare which one generates better with the flap design you have. Don't know if this makes any sense but the current arrangement made me think of Tesla Valve as the above flaps might simultaneously let air in and push air from below out.

author: @swrekcfest
text:
Maybe to improve the efficiency

1.  turn the flaps upside down so the air doesn’t lose as much energy going around down in circles and just goes straight up into the generator.

2. Link the flaps together on each separate vent, add a spring system and put some tension on it just to make it easier for the air to push the flaps and produce a bit more and so they also return into position when there is no wind blowing on that face.

3. Try to add something like a 90 degree pipe on top of the fan with the exhaust facing away from the wind , so that maybe it creates a low air pressure zone or maybe like a very small vacuum to help the device produce even more consistently and efficiently.

Just some thoughts for people who might want to build it . 
Thanks Rob for the awesome video !

author: @rsummers1974ify
text:
Another Great video!
Robert just before I watched this video I was vacuuming my work desk and there was a 3/8 drill bit laying next to the Router. As I passed the vacuum above it. A extremely loud pitch came from it resonating my grange. I tried different drill bits and a different tone and pitch. From my observation it seems on the style of flutes and length and diameter. Which is to be expected. But could these tones be used to occalate something to produce electricity from wind without rotation?

replies.author: @lancealderman2755
replies.text:
Something of a similar idea is a linear generator (imagine a rod with magnets surrounded by coils for instance) with the rod connected to the ground and then a spring on one end and something that blows in the wind (like a tree branch) on the other.
How that is similar to producing power through a sound vibration I can't exactly articulate. Haha. Instead of a tree branch it would have to be a plate of some sort to catch vibrations with the rod connected to the center.
I came to the conclusion that my idea would be dangerous and not produce much power... which is probably the case with catching sound, but I think ideas will lead us to something some day.

A Modern Day Practical Application of a similar theory was Patented more than 30 years ago , AND IT WORKED MAGNIFICENTLY!

I sent the Patent Info to you before , if you would like it again , please let me know.

Regards.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @AG-yb1lm
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Title : Tornado-Type Wind Turbine

Patent No US4070131A
Application : 1975
Inventor : James T. Yen
Assignee : Grumman Aerospace Corp

I had access to the corporate library back-then , extraordinary , all the engineering & prototyping was completed by Yen and Grumman.

They determined the diameter to height ratio. The temperature difference between the base and top of the silo to effectuate natural air flow. They built negative pressure louvers to allow air to be sucked in as the 'tornado' formed , etc..

You could use recycled heat at the base to cause cyclonic rotation in times of low outside ambient air flow. 

Have fun - would love to see you build it! 

Regards - let me know you received this!

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: The problem is airflow you need cold air sucked up through the bottom but if the wind does blow you are losing air pressure out the bottom so the bottom row gets swing flaps or something that you can lock when the wind is blowing.

replies.author: @stl7694
replies.text: I don't think I explained the concept correctly.  The rising heat in the column acts like a localized thermal updraft.  The air pressure in the column would pull additional air volume through the baffles along the entire distance.  When the wind blows the baffles only open if the pressure outside is greater than the pressure inside meaning you won't lose anything.

author: @pattayaguideorg
text: I was at a public swimming pool one day taking a dip, and on the edge of the pool was a red safety cone, I could see the waves all the kids were making moving just below the cone, the idea hit me that if the cone was below the bottom of the wave, as it moved from top to bottom it would suck air in and out through the top of the cone, I then imagined a 2 way blade system that could turn an shaft the same direction, on top of the cone, whether air was being sucked in or pushed out of the cone, I drew some designs up on paper, they looked interesting but that's as far as it got. the inside of the cone, because of the angle, acts like a compressor as the water rises and a vacuum is created when the wave drops, I see similar idea's kicking around now. good vid mate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice thinking mate - there are designs based on this they use large concrete structures at just below sea level and the air does exactly what you say

author: @fireballloadout
text: It does look really interesting but would it be affected by how high it is? My thinking is that the wind if it came in the lower sections might not be as powerful going upward then the wind coming near the top sections

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe but if it was trying to escape it would slam the vents shut and end up helping in building up pressure wouldn't it?

replies.author: @fireballloadout
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering yes it would build up pressure I imagine but when it hits the slam surface some of the wind is going to go upward and some of it's going to go downward, if the winds comes near the bottom it's going to hit the wall with some of that is going to go downward and spiral around the ground to go upward losing some of its momentum and power in the process most likely. As weird as it sounds maybe putting a PC fan near the bottom would help the air move quicker upward and not lose the momentum of the wind when it goes downward

replies.author: @fireballloadout
replies.text: Even in the original design picture that you showed off, there was a turbine in the center of it that would capture the air and help it to go upward. A good way of visualizing it or at least a way I'm doing it is think of a stream of water hitting a wall, some of its going to go upward and some of it going to go downward

author: @xlerb_again_to_music7908
text:
Hmmm. I notice the wind must be deflected down by the slats, then reverse to rise so losing momentum. Why not lift the main structure up and the fan goes in the bottom? This may need reversed slats (again in a square) so the wind exits on the reverse direction side. My intent is to keep the airflow momentum thus improving speed hence energy transfer.

Another idea would be to place some cooling liquid eg water at the bottom; as that loses heat due to the exiting airflow across it, the thermal drop / difference could drive a Stirling engine. Anybody looked at wind-cooling to power a Stirling engine yet?? Any maths on that one?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the fan is only there to simulate wind - in real life the wind comes from all over - so you could position the wind to come in from the bottom but that would use 'trumpet' shapes and that has been done too
